title: Generating Non-Decomposable Maps with Differentiable Semidefinite Programming
authors: Angela Rosy Morgillo, Davide Poderini, Fabio Anselmi, Fabio Benatti, Massimiliano F. Sacchi, Chiara Macchiavello
abstract: Positive maps that are not decomposable are a key resource in entanglement theory because they can detect bound entangled states, yet systematic methods for constructing them remain limited. We introduce an optimization framework based on differentiable semidefinite programming (SDP) for generating positive non-decomposable maps under flexible structural constraints on their Choi matrices. The method combines SDP-based certificates of non-decomposability and positivity with gradient-based optimization, enabling a systematic search over maps with different input and output dimensions. Within this framework, we generate previously unknown numerical examples, identify a parametrized family of maps arising from masked Choi matrices, and construct real non-decomposable maps. We further show that the same approach can be adapted to explore open questions in quantum information theory, including the PPT square conjecture and recently proposed eigenvalue bounds for 2-positive trace-preserving maps.
summary: The authors present a new optimization method using differentiable semidefinite programming to systematically generate positive non-decomposable quantum maps. This framework allows for the discovery of new map families and provides numerical evidence for long-standing conjectures in quantum information theory. It effectively automates the search for complex mathematical structures used to detect bound entanglement.
main_problem: The difficulty of systematically constructing positive, non-decomposable linear maps, which are essential for detecting bound entangled states.
main_result: The development of a differentiable SDP framework that successfully generates new numerical examples of non-decomposable maps, identifies a new parametrized family of maps, and provides numerical evidence for the PPT square conjecture.
method: An optimization framework using differentiable semidefinite programming (SDP) layers within a gradient-based loop (Adam optimizer) to minimize a loss function based on positivity and non-decomposability certificates.
model_or_system: Linear maps between finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces, represented via the Choi-Jamiołkowski isomorphism using Choi matrices.
key_observables: Positivity certificates (via k-symmetric extensions), non-decomposability witnesses (zeta_1), and trace-preservation properties.
important_parameters: Input and output dimensions (d, d'), extension order (k), and loss function hyperparameters (epsilon, gamma, omega, nu).
main_assumptions: The use of k-symmetric extensions provides a sufficient but not necessary condition for positivity, which may exclude some valid maps.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the optimization framework flow; Figure 2 displays Choi matrices and masks; Figure 3 shows real non-decomposable maps; Figure 4 shows convergence relative to hyperparameters; Figures 5-7 illustrate the evolution of loss functions and SDP objectives during training.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of non-decomposable maps, presents the differentiable SDP optimization framework, details the loss function engineering and masking techniques, demonstrates results including new map families and conjecture testing, and discusses limitations and future work.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful new numerical tool for exploring the geometry of quantum channels and entanglement, which is directly relevant to understanding the structure of open quantum systems and entanglement detection.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic overview of the optimization framework. A parametrized Choi matrix C, optionally constrained by a mask, is processed by two SDP layers. The first evaluates the witness-based objective ζ1, which certifies non-decomposability when it becomes negative. The second evaluates ζk, a positivity certificate based on PPT k-symmetric extensions. These quantities are combined in the loss function (21), possibly together with additional penalty terms implementing further structural constraints. Minimizing this loss with Adam yields an updated Choi matrix ˜C whose associated map is positive and non-decomposable.
FIG. 2. Example of a Choi matrix associated with a positive non-decomposable map on C4 ⊗C4.

FIG. 3. Examples of real Choi matrices corresponding to non-decomposable maps on C2 ⊗C4, obtained using different masking patterns in the optimization procedure. Nonzero entries indicate the locations selected by the masks.
FIG. 4. Number of epochs required to reach convergence, defined as the point at which the loss function in Eq. (21) becomes zero, as a function of the hyperparameters γ and ϵ.

Fig. 4 shows the number of epochs required for conver- gence, defined as the point at which the loss in Eq. (21) reaches zero, as a function of γ and ϵ. For most param- eter choices, convergence is achieved in fewer than 400 epochs. There are, however, combinations with small γ and large ϵ for which convergence is not observed. This is consistent with the interpretation of the loss: increasing ϵ imposes a stronger requirement on non-decomposability, while decreasing γ weakens the relative pressure toward positivity, making it more difficult to identify a positive non-decomposable map. We also examine how ϵ and γ affect the loss trajec- tory during training. Figs. 5 and 6 show the evolution
